12: This Python script defines a single function called greet, which takes a person's name
13: and returns a personalized greeting. The @tool() decorator above this function
14: automatically registers it as a tool that an AI or another program can use. The
15: function's documentation string and type hints are used by FastMCP to tell the Agent
16: how the tool works, what inputs it needs, and what it will return.
17: When the script is executed, it starts the FastMCP server, which listens for requests
18: on localhost:8000. This makes the greet function available as a network service. An
19: agent could then be configured to connect to this server and use the greet tool to
20: generate greetings as part of a larger task. The server runs continuously until it is
22: Consuming the FastMCP Server with an ADK Agent
23: An ADK agent can be set up as an MCP client to use a running FastMCP server. This
24: requires configuring HttpServerParameters with the FastMCP server's network
26: A tool_filter parameter can be included to restrict the agent's tool usage to specific
27: tools offered by the server, such as 'greet'. When prompted with a request like "Greet
28: John Doe," the agent's embedded LLM identifies the 'greet' tool available via MCP,
29: invokes it with the argument "John Doe," and returns the server's response. This
30: process demonstrates the integration of user-defined tools exposed through MCP
32: To establish this configuration, an agent file (e.g., agent.py located in
